speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five, the survivor is Samuel Friedman. What would you tell the people in Friedland, when they asked you about your past?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I must have had a story to tell them-- school, education, family. But I can't remember it, what I said. I think I made up a story, something.

speaker: interviewer
text: But nobody was really asking me. And I wasn't asking anybody. I couldn't go to-- to start talking with some Polish men who was working in the factory, what is your schooling, your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was no point of talking about this. We were talking about current affairs. We were talking about-- about the war, and the war, and the war. This was the topic all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't ask them. The Frenchmen didn't ask me where I came from either. I didn't ask them. One came from Clermont-Ferrand. I didn't ask him what he did in Clermont-Ferrand. So this wasn't the case.

speaker: interviewee
text: But thinking in hindsight, and seeing the two men-- one of them we called father, because he was father figure. So much older, he was, so much more clever. [LAUGHS] How he didn't know that I am Jewish, this is ridiculous. Absolutely ridiculous. With a name Friedman, on top of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think these two and maybe some more knew that I am Jewish. But they shut up. They never opened their mouths, never said nothing. Maybe they protected me even. I am quite sure they protected me. Who was the mayor of Friedland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know. But I knew the Chief of the Police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about him.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Chief of the Police was-- the bastard, unbelievable. The Poles, who wanted to go for a holiday home and got the permission from the factory had to go to the Chief of the Police, Hauptmann Stohler-- [LAUGHS] I know the name, Hauptmann Stohler, Captain Stohler-- and then leave a deposit of 200 marks. So they will be-- so he will be sure they come back. Because nobody-- and nobody was worried about the 200 marks or something. Still, they went to Captain Stohler. And he was shouting at them, you! You don't come back! We will kill you or something. You have to leave $200-- 200 marks as a deposit. So everybody knew what to say. Yes, Captain Stohler, it's a lot of money. Because they didn't want to tell him that you could lose 200 or 500 in one night's card-- cards. This is a lot of money, Hauptmann Stohler. But still, I saved this to see my family. When the time came that Mr. Friedman got the permission to go to Poland to see his family, he was in trouble. Because he didn't know where to go. But I had correspondence with one of the girls from the teacher's college. She was living in Brody. And I was writing to her mainly to have some correspondence, letters to-- letters to-- to get from-- from somewhere. Because I never had a letter from anybody. So I wrote to this girl. And in the meantime, she changed her address. And she was in Germany, working in a factory. She was sent or something, a Polish girl. And I wrote her, listen, maybe you can rent me a room in Schweidnitz. This was today's Poland, Schweidnitz. And I will come. And I  will stay two weeks in the room. And we will meet. We will see or something. And she-- she was living with a friend or a boyfriend or something. But she rented me a room. And so the time came that I had to go for holidays. And I organized this, that I will go to Schweidnitz. And I will stay two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was my holiday. Nobody will ask me, how is the family? It's OK. So I went to Hauptmann Stohler with the 200 marks. And I ask Hauptmann Stohler, I want to go to-- I have the permission to go to see my family. Ah, you have to leave the 200. And the story, yeah, [INAUDIBLE], it's a lot of money. I've [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, so he showed me to the policeman to organize the papers. Then I went to the policemen. Hauptmann Stohler is back in the room. I like you. You will leave only 100-mark deposit. Oh, danke shoen, thank you very much, Hauptmann Stohler. Thank you very much. [LAUGHS] A minute later, Hauptmann Stohler is back. And you believe it or not, you have not to leave a deposit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I like you. And you don't leave. But if you don't come, we will shoot you. How they will shoot me, I don't come? This was his way of living. He hanged himself later, when the Russians came in, by the way--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --Hauptmann Stohler. When I told my friends, the Poles, this story, this was the most exciting story in town for weeks. Waclav-- this was me in Polish-- is going on family holidays? And he didn't have to leave the deposit, the 200 marks, unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I'm laughing to-- to this day about it. Anyway, I went on holidays. And I went by train. And I came to Berlin. And it was bombing of Berlin. This was unbelievable. For two or three hours we were waiting in trains somewhere outside Berlin. We couldn't get through. We couldn't pass. We couldn't do nothing. Until later on the gangs, the rival gangs, came to repair the-- only the-- to-- to pass the trains. And so we passed the train. And I says-- so I went down to Silesia, to the south.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the way to Silesia, at night-- it's night, maybe two o'clock in the morning-- the door opens. The lights-- a light on. [GERMAN]. Which is Gestapo. You like it? So the Gestapo came [? through. ?] But I wasn't interested in this at all. Because my papers were 100-- were 100% right from the-- from the [INAUDIBLE], or the employment office. So I showed my paper. Gute nacht, danke shoen. And that's all. But this is-- this was happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, I came to the town. And the rest of this is not of very much interest. I was bored to death. The girl didn't have much time for me because she was working. And she had a boyfriend anyway. And so I was living there and going around and looking, and whatever I bought, a few things, souvenirs. And then time came, and I went home. And everybody had the story, how fine it was. I was here. And I was there. But they didn't ask me many questions. I can't remember, I can't recall that I was telling to anybody, my vacation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had to been away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us more about the Nazi propaganda that you witnessed in Friedland.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- see, I am afraid I have nothing to say. What Nazi propaganda? To whom?

speaker: interviewer
text: The one you may have read in the

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. What you read in the paper, you know what was the Nazi thing. It's nothing-- nothing new. [INAUDIBLE] but there is writing about the war, and the war, and the war. Everything was around the war. It was not very much Nazi propaganda.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is Nazi propaganda? Nazi propaganda was probably before the-- before the elections in Germany. But not during the war. During the war was-- they were telling their story-- their story about war, about the-- what's happening on the fronts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we knew our story. We were just looking at the locations. If the heavy artillery was south of Berlin, this was very good news. So I can't say about-- anything about Nazi propaganda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you aware of the Friedland camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friedland camp? What camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a camp in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: A camp? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: A concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Absolutely not. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a camp of prisoners of war, the French prisoners of war. Not concentration camp. Not in our area. We knew about Brandenburg. And we knew about Dachau. But we didn't know about Auschwitz. Or-- or-- we knew Buchenwald. But we didn't know about Treblinka or-- or some other thing. We didn't know. It was nowhere in the papers. The BBC, what you got is only the front news, what's happening on the front. They didn't bother about the Jews or something. I-- I didn't hear anything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about Dachau, that you mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that there is a concentration camp in Dachau, where people are [? had, ?] Germans and Jews, and others. And the conditions are terrible. That's all. We didn't know anything about. They didn't let to-- they didn't let, in the papers, even to mention it. So if you didn't have it in the papers, something. Even if this is not true, even if they lie about something, you can say, ah, this is a lie. Maybe it's not exactly so. But they didn't mention it. So you didn't have to compare it with anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the civilians? Would they have had a chance of knowing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't speak to the civilians. They didn't speak to us. No way. They wouldn't open they mouth, the civilians. They didn't know who and what, nothing. They would-- they wouldn't talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: 28 of April, '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew that the front is approaching. I think this was the 2nd Belorussian front, Russian. And we knew because we heard the artillery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day when the artillery was intense, it's heavy, we decided, we-- it means a group of people, Polish-- we decided to run away from town and to hide somewhere in the fields, just for security. And we found out that the railway-- railway's large pipes, cement pipes, for drainage.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was larger than sitting men. You could sit inside. It was a bit of water inside, but this doesn't bother us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went there. It was out of town, a small town. A kilometer from town, you are out of town, no houses, no nothing. So we sat the whole night. And the artillery was really heavy. And we saw even the Katyusha launchers firing. Because at night, you could see them fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was-- everything, it was somewhere else, somewhere far-- further up. One bomb only, one rocket only came very close, but didn't explode. Still, it wasn't so close. But we saw it. So a bit of luck. And in the morning, we started looking what's happening. And we went out of the-- of the trenches. And we were a group, maybe 10, maybe 8 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then slowly we approach-- we move towards the town. And then two cavalry men came running towards us. And they ask us in Russian, where are the Germans? So I came. I told them in Russian, we are Poles who are living here. We were working here. And the Russians went this and this way. And so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, good, good. He didn't bother about it. He wasn't happy. He wasn't dancing with us or kissing us, nothing. Oh, oh, good, go home. Which we went. So we knew that the Russians are in. So we came to town. In town, we saw tanks full of linen and bed linen and linen and-- and houseware and everything. You couldn't see the tank. It was so full. Everywhere, on the cannons, hanging washing. This was ridiculous. But they were taking from the houses and taking this home. Everything was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we organized ourselves as well. We went to a shop. We took some-- I can't remember what-- bits and pieces which are irrelevant today. But still, it was very important then, because we didn't very much clothing, warm clothing. So we took it from the shops. And then later on, they burnt down the town, the Russians, completely. It was burnt out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we started moving. We had a horse from somewhere, because it was a larger group of Poles, and a-- a wagon, horse-driven wagon. And we started moving towards Poland, with all of the belongings and bits and pieces.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we came, slowly, to the river Oder. And the bridge on the river Oder was in pieces. So we had to wait for the-- for the river-- for the bridge they made of-- from-- how do you call it? The Artificial bridge.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we crossed, eventually, to Poland. And then we followed to start looking for families. And then was the big tragedy, that we couldn't find anybody. And I went to the committee of Kalisz Jews in Kalisz, to see the list who has survived. And then, only then, we knew about what's happening. Real-- the real thing. Because during the war, you knew and you didn't. You couldn't believe it. If you had something, you-- it was too-- too impossible to believe. And now, we realize they are not here, and they are not here. I found my cousin, a lady, and her sister, in the list. And I contacted them. I-- And they told me that they were with my parents till the end of-- of the ghetto. And then they were hiding somewhere in a Polish village, on a farm. And they knew-- they told me that my parents received some letters from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your parents and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is what I know. They were taken from Warsaw ghetto to Treblinka. And then perished in Treblinka. But I am not sure 100%. This is what I was told by my cousin. Because it happened with the-- the majority of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did they pass away?

speaker: interviewer
text: What month or season?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I wouldn't know. Season, I would say, summer-- summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have established myself at my wife's place. Because I met, in the meantime, my wife. And her parents were living in Krakow. And I didn't have where to go at all. So we decided to stay for awhile, together. We married in a ceremony. We had a very good wedding ceremony, and-- well, a civil ceremony. And then we went to a-- to the-- to the new German-occupied territory by Poles, near Szczecin. And we got a flat, a very nice flat, fully furnished with German things. Because the German ran away, so we took it over. And I got a position in the city council. And-- but very soon I saw that Jews are not very much welcomed anyway. So I changed my name again, quickly, from Samuel to Waclav, my first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you mean, they were not welcome?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, the commanding officer of the militia, you know, in Poland there were not police, in the Communist Poland, the militia, he came to ask me, what kind of name have you got? Who are you? Where are you from?

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, listen, you are a police commissioner from a Communist country, from Polish-- new Polish Republic. What are asking such a question? Oh, there are so many Jews here. And so then I knew it's not so good.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, came the Kielce Pogrom. You must be aware, probably. Kielce Pogrom, this was the end of it. Then we knew we are not welcome. They will kill us. And let's move out. In the meantime, I got a letter from my cousin, this lady whom I met in Poland, from Vienna. And she told me, you come, better quickly, and get out. So my wife and myself, we packed up. And we went through the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --it was a Jewish organization, Bricha, which is part of Haganah. I-- I enrolled. Or they-- they send me through some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --other organization from Poland, by train, to Germany. And we arrived in Germany, with-- to Frankfurt. Then later we went to Bogenhausen and other camps. And we stayed there until we-- until we could migrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your impression of post-war Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- we saw in-- Kassel. We went to Kassel, it was so bombed. It was absolutely not recognizable. It was in powder. And then other cities were, more or less, intact. Like Frankfort, there wasn't very much damage. And well, we didn't have any contact with Germans. We didn't see Germans. We didn't talk to them. We didn't see them. Absolutely no one there. In the camps, in our-- in the-- this is DP camps, we had a Jewish organization and the Jewish distribution of food, which was the main concern of people, food. And the other concern was how to migrate somewhere, how to get out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But with Germans, who talked to Germans? I never met Germans. I never talked to them. In the DP camps, what did you know about the numbers of the Jews that had been killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you suspect?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were shocked. Everybody was shocked. And everybody was looking how to organize themselves, how to get out of it, how to go somewhere, how to start new life. This was the main thing in the camps. We left the camps with-- with the Bricha. From Germany, it was organized to go to Austria. And from-- to Austria, we went by foot, on foot, crossing the border and the rivers and things like this. And from-- from Bad Reichenhall, in Germany, we went to a camp near the Italian border. And we crossed the Alps on foot with a group of maybe 200 people, children, older people. We crossed the Alps in July, in snow. WE can imagine how high was it. This climbing wasn't so hard like this descending. It wasn't so hard, like-- to get off the mountain, it was terrible. You couldn't-- you couldn't-- because of the snow, because of the cold. And then we came to a camp called [INAUDIBLE]. This was Haganah camp. This was organized so that this was on the Italian side of the border. But the Italians had a agreement with the Haganah. Because of the British intelligence interfering with this migration, they didn't want the Jews to come to Italy and go put the pressure on Palestine. So the arrangement between the Haganah and the Italian Carabinieri was every day we will send, at daytime, some group of 50, 60 men back to Austria, so the British intelligence will be shown that they-- we are sending them back. At night, we will put everybody on trucks and over to Italy. So I was one of these 50 men who went to-- from Italy to Austria. Somewhere on the way to Austria, I run away. I went up somewhere, the mountain. I couldn't get out. But the only sign, how-- of direction was the river. A small, very narrow river was going south. And so I was following the river somewhere. And anyway, I arrived one of the first in the camp, back. And the others came late during the night. Late night, we were put on the trucks and to the Italian border. And over to spread through the small Italian towns in every railway station, three, four people. And we were told you go to Milan. And in Milan, everybody knows that you have no money and no ticket. And every train conductor will take you to [? Vianen ?] [INAUDIBLE], five. And this is the center of the Jewish immigration. So we came to the Milan station. But you could see the Jews coming from-- it was 1 of August, '46, '47-- hot in-- in Italy. And we were all clothes in the very heavy clothes, from Austria, from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody knew who we are. Anyway, we arrived in [? Vianen. ?] [? Vianen, ?] we go to the bed, myself and my wife, in the middle of a huge room with 300 beds, probably. One, you could only put your head on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't like it. And then I heard that the train is going tonight to Bari, where the conditions are much better. So I enrolled. Somehow I got on the train. And we went by train to Bari. And we landed in Bari. Later, we landed in Bari Palese. And then later, we were transferred to Barletta. And this was a huge camp in Barletta where I stayed a year and a half in Italy. My daughter was born there. And I was working as a teacher at the local school-- local-- the camp school. I have pictures from the school with the other teachers and the headmaster. And I was teaching in Yiddish. Physics, and mathematics, in Yiddish. Because the children didn't know any other language. They didn't know any language. And we were waiting for migrate-- for State of Israel to be established, which it was. We didn't go to Cyprus, because we had a little child. So with children, they didn't accept to go to Cyprus. You know the story of Cyprus. And then we arrived in Haifa, on [INAUDIBLE], '48. This was the 26 of October, '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that day. What did it mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. When we came to [? Naples, ?] with our little daughter and the-- the transport of fellows-- fellow people, we went to the port to see our ship. And we saw huge ships that day. And between the huge ships-- ships was a small boat. This was the boat. This was the [INAUDIBLE]. On the [INAUDIBLE], we had four days. I was sitting on the stairs. My wife was non-existent from sickness, from seasickness. Some other people were sick. And I was keeping the little daughter and feeding her special biscuits, Italian biscuits. We had special baby biscuits. So this was the passage to-- to Haifa. And then approaching Haifa, we saw the Mount Carmel, which is still standing. And everybody was starting singing the Hatikvah, and [INAUDIBLE]. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --we arrived and we went straight to the [INAUDIBLE] in [INAUDIBLE]. This was the [HEBREW] camp, which means the new arrivals.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll change the tape.
